questions.q: Match each ring with its prime spectrum $\Spec(R)$ as a set of points.
left: $\{(0)\}\cup\{(p):p\text{ prime}\}$ — one generic point plus countably many closed points.
Two closed points — corresponding to the prime divisors of $12$.
$\{(0)\}\cup\{(x-a):a\in k\}$ — one generic point plus one closed point for each $a\in k$.
questions.hint: $\Spec(R)=\{\text{prime ideals}\}$. Fields have only $(0)$; $\mathbb Z/n$'s primes correspond to prime divisors of $n$; $k[x]$ over algebraically closed $k$ has primes $(0)$ and $(x-a)$ for $a\in k$.
questions.explain: $\Spec\mathbb Z$: primes $(0)$ (generic) and $(p)$ (closed) for each rational prime $p$. $\Spec\mathbb Z/12$: only primes $(2),(3)$ survive — note $(0)$ is not prime in $\mathbb Z/12$ since $\mathbb Z/12$ has zero-divisors. $\Spec{}k[x]$ with $k=\bar k$: primes are $(0)$ (generic) and $(x-a)$ for $a\in k$ (closed). $\Spec\mathbb Q$: only $(0)$ — every field is a one-point scheme.

questions.q: How many points does $\Spec\mathbb{Z}/12\mathbb{Z}$ have? (Count prime ideals of $\mathbb Z/12\mathbb Z$.)
questions.explain: Prime ideals of $\mathbb Z/12$ correspond to primes $p$ with $p\mid 12$, namely $p=2$ and $p=3$. So there are $2$ points.

questions.q: The Zariski-closed sets of $\Spec(R)$ are:
choices: $V(I)=\{\mathfrak p : I\subseteq \mathfrak p\}$ for ideals $I\subseteq R$
questions.explain: The vanishing loci $V(I)=\{\mathfrak p\supseteq I\}$ define the Zariski topology. They are closed under arbitrary intersection and finite union.

hard.q: Consider $R=k[x,y]/(xy)$. Which of the following correctly describes $\Spec(R)$ and its irreducible components?
choices: It is irreducible with a single generic point $(xy)$
It has two irreducible components corresponding to $(x)$ and $(y)$, meeting at the maximal ideal $(x,y)$
It is smooth everywhere and has one component
It has no closed points because $k[x,y]/(xy)$ is not Noetherian
hard.explain: $xy=0$ means any prime contains $x$ or $y$. The minimal primes are $(x)$ and $(y)$, giving two irreducible components (the two coordinate axes). They meet at the closed point $(x,y)$, making $\Spec(R)$ reducible.

hard.q: How many points does $\Spec(\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1))$ have that lie over the prime $(2)\subset\mathbb{Z}$? (Count prime ideals of $\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$ above $2$.)
hard.explain: Over $(2)$: $\mathbb{F}_2[x]/(x^2+1)=\mathbb{F}_2[x]/(x+1)^2$ since $x^2+1=(x+1)^2$ in characteristic $2$. This quotient has a unique prime $(x+1)$, so there is exactly $1$ prime above $2$.

hard.q: Let $I=(x^2,y)\subset k[x,y]$. Which statement about $V(I)\subset\Spec(k[x,y])$ is correct?
choices: $V(I)=V((x^2,y))$ consists of two distinct closed points
$V(I)$ is empty because $I$ contains a unit
$V(I)=V((x,y^2))$ since the two ideals generate the same ring
hard.explain: The radical $\sqrt{(x^2,y)}=(x,y)$ because any prime containing $x^2$ must contain $x$. Hence $V(I)=V(\sqrt{I})=V((x,y))$, the single closed point $(x,y)$. The scheme-theoretic structure differs (nilpotents exist), but the underlying set is the same.

questions.q: Match each piece of structure on the affine scheme $X=\Spec(R)$ with the algebraic object it corresponds to.
left: Sections on a distinguished open $\mathcal O_X(D(f))$ for $f\in R$
Stalk $\mathcal O_{X,\mathfrak p}$ at a prime $\mathfrak p$
right: Localization $R_f = R[f^{-1}]$ — invert $f$ to allow $1/f$ as a function on $D(f)$.
Localization $R_{\mathfrak p}$ — a local ring with maximal ideal $\mathfrak pR_{\mathfrak p}$.
$\operatorname{Frac}(R/\mathfrak p) = R_{\mathfrak p}/\mathfrak pR_{\mathfrak p}$ — the residue field of the local ring.
questions.hint: The structure sheaf of $\Spec(R)$ is engineered so that global sections give back $R$, distinguished-open sections invert the corresponding element, stalks localize at the prime, and residue fields are quotient + Frac.
questions.explain: On $X=\Spec(R)$: $\mathcal O_X(X)=R$, $\mathcal O_X(D(f))=R_f$, $\mathcal O_{X,\mathfrak p}=R_{\mathfrak p}$, and the residue field is $\kappa(\mathfrak p)=\operatorname{Frac}(R/\mathfrak p)$. The first three are the engine of the equivalence $\operatorname{CommRing}^{\mathrm{op}}\cong\mathrm{AffSch}$; the residue field captures the value of a 'function' at the point $\mathfrak p$.

questions.q: For the distinguished open $D(f)=\{\mathfrak p : f\notin \mathfrak p\}\subset \Spec(R)$, the ring of sections is:
questions.explain: $\mathcal O(D(f))=R_f$: inverting $f$ allows $1/f$ as a regular function exactly on the locus where $f$ does not vanish.

questions.q: The residue field $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$ at the generic point $\mathfrak p=(0)$ of $\Spec\mathbb{Z}$ is $\mathbb Q$. What is its characteristic?
questions.explain: $\mathbb Q$ has characteristic $0$. By contrast $\kappa((p))=\mathbb F_p$ at a closed point has characteristic $p$ — different points can have residue fields of different characteristics.

hard.q: For $R=k[x]/(x^2)$ (the dual numbers), $\Spec(R)$ has a single point $\mathfrak m=(x)$. The stalk $\mathcal{O}_{\mathfrak m}$ equals $R_{\mathfrak m}$. Which statement about sections on the unique non-empty open set is correct?
choices: $\mathcal{O}(\Spec{}R)=k$ because sheaves always see reduced rings
$\mathcal{O}(\Spec{}R)=k[x]$ since we must invert all elements
The structure sheaf is undefined when $R$ has nilpotents
hard.explain: Global sections of $\mathcal{O}$ on $\Spec(R)$ equal $R$ itself. The nilpotent $x$ is a legitimate global section; scheme theory allows non-reduced rings and their nilpotents encode infinitesimal thickening.

hard.q: Let $f:\Spec(B)\to\Spec(A)$ be the morphism induced by a ring map $\phi:A\to B$. The image of a prime $\mathfrak q\subset B$ under $f$ is:
choices: $\phi^{-1}(\mathfrak q)$, the contraction of $\mathfrak q$ to $A$
hard.explain: The continuous map on spectra sends $\mathfrak q$ to $f(\mathfrak q)=\phi^{-1}(\mathfrak q)=\{a\in A:\phi(a)\in\mathfrak q\}$, which is prime in $A$ because $\phi$ is a ring homomorphism.

hard.q: Let $R=\mathbb{Z}[i]=\mathbb{Z}[x]/(x^2+1)$. The prime $(3)\subset\mathbb{Z}$ splits in $R$ as $(3)=(3,i-?)(3,i+?)$ because $x^2+1=(x-1)(x+1)\pmod{3}$ is incorrect; in fact $x^2+1$ is irreducible mod $3$. How many primes of $R$ lie over $(3)$?
hard.explain: $x^2+1\pmod{3}$: checking $x=0,1,2$ gives $1,2,2$ — no root, so $x^2+1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_3$. Thus $(3)$ remains prime in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ (it is inert), and there is exactly $1$ prime of $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ above $3$.

questions.q: The projective line $\mathbb P^1_k$ is built by gluing two copies of $\mathbb A^1_k=\Spec{}k[x]$ and $\Spec{}k[y]$ along the overlap $\{x\ne 0\}\cong\{y\ne 0\}$ via:
questions.explain: The transition is $y=1/x$: on the overlap $\Spec{}k[x,x^{-1}]=\Spec{}k[y,y^{-1}]$ with $y$ inverse to $x$. The two origins become $0$ and $\infty$.

questions.q: How many closed points does $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb F_q}$ have over a finite field $\mathbb F_q$? Give the answer for $q=5$.
questions.explain: $|\mathbb P^1(\mathbb F_q)|=q+1$. For $q=5$ that is $5+1=6$ closed points: the $5$ values of $x\in\mathbb F_5$ plus the point at infinity.

questions.q: Which statement about $\mathbb P^1_k$ is TRUE?
choices: $\mathbb P^1_k$ is not affine but is covered by two affine opens
$\mathbb P^1_k$ has no affine open subsets
questions.explain: $\mathbb P^1_k$ is the classic non-affine scheme. Global sections of $\mathcal O$ are just $k$ (too few for an affine of positive dimension), yet it is covered by two affine charts $\mathbb A^1_k$.

hard.q: To glue two affine schemes $X_1=\Spec(A_1)$ and $X_2=\Spec(A_2)$ along open subsets $U_{12}\subset X_1$ and $U_{21}\subset X_2$ via an isomorphism $\varphi:U_{12}\xrightarrow{\sim}U_{21}$, which cocycle condition must hold when gluing three affines $X_1,X_2,X_3$?
choices: No condition is needed; any collection of pairwise gluing data yields a scheme
The overlap isomorphisms must satisfy $\varphi_{31}=\varphi_{32}\circ\varphi_{21}$ on $U_{12}\cap U_{13}$
Each $\varphi_{ij}$ must be the identity map
The triple overlaps must be empty
hard.explain: The cocycle condition $\varphi_{ki}=\varphi_{kj}\circ\varphi_{ji}$ on triple overlaps ensures the local isomorphisms are consistent; without it the resulting space may fail to be a scheme (or may not even be well-defined as a ringed space).

hard.q: The projective space $\mathbb{P}^n_k$ is covered by $n+1$ standard affine charts $D_+(x_i)\cong\mathbb{A}^n_k$. For $n=3$, how many affine charts are in the standard cover?
hard.explain: $\mathbb{P}^3_k$ is covered by $D_+(x_0),D_+(x_1),D_+(x_2),D_+(x_3)$, giving $n+1=4$ affine charts.

hard.q: Which is a correct counterexample showing that a scheme obtained by gluing affines need not be separated?
choices: The affine line with doubled origin: glue two copies of $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ along $\mathbb{A}^1_k\setminus\{0\}$ via the identity; the diagonal is not closed
$\mathbb{A}^2_k$ — it fails the valuative criterion of separatedness
hard.hint: Think of a classic Hausdorff-failure analogue in point-set topology.
hard.explain: The line with doubled origin is the standard non-separated scheme: two copies of $\mathbb{A}^1_k$ glued on the complement of the origin. The two origins cannot be separated by open sets, and the diagonal is not closed in the product, violating separatedness.

questions.q: Closed subschemes of $\Spec R$ correspond bijectively to:
questions.explain: Each ideal $I\subseteq R$ gives a closed subscheme $\Spec(R/I)\hookrightarrow\Spec{}R$, and distinct ideals give distinct subschemes (even when their radicals — hence underlying sets — agree). Radical ideals pick out only the reduced closed subschemes.
questions.hint: Different ideals with the same $\sqrt{I}$ can still define different subschemes.

questions.q: The ring $R=k[x,y]/(xy)$ has how many irreducible components when viewed as $\Spec{}R$?
questions.explain: The minimal primes of $k[x,y]/(xy)$ are $(x)$ and $(y)$, corresponding to the two coordinate axes. So $\Spec{}R$ is the union of two irreducible components meeting at the origin.

questions.q: The ideal $I=(x^2,xy,y^2)\subset k[x,y]$ has radical $\sqrt I = (x,y)$. As a $k$-vector space the quotient $k[x,y]/I$ has what dimension (the length of the fat point)?
questions.explain: A $k$-basis of $k[x,y]/(x^2,xy,y^2)$ is $\{1,x,y\}$, so the quotient has $k$-dimension $3$. The reduced point has length $1$, so this is a non-reduced length-$3$ scheme concentrated at the origin.

hard.q: Let $R = k[x,y]/(x^2-y^3)$ (cuspidal cubic). Which statement is TRUE?
choices: $R$ is a field, so $\Spec{}R$ is a single reduced point
hard.explain: $x^2-y^3$ is irreducible in $k[x,y]$ (it is a prime, since $k[x,y]$ is a UFD and the polynomial is irreducible), so $R=k[x,y]/(x^2-y^3)$ is an integral domain — reduced, and $\Spec{}R$ is irreducible with generic point $(0)$. The cusp at the origin is a singular point, but singular $\ne$ non-reduced.

hard.q: The reduced subscheme underlying the closed subscheme $V(I)\subset\Spec{}R$ is:
hard.explain: Passing to the radical $\sqrt I$ kills all nilpotents in the quotient. The reduced subscheme with the same underlying set $V(I)$ is $\Spec(R/\sqrt I)$; the inclusion $\sqrt I\supseteq I$ induces a natural closed immersion $\Spec(R/\sqrt I)\hookrightarrow\Spec(R/I)$.

hard.q: How many minimal primes does $R=\mathbb Z[x]/(6x) = \mathbb Z[x]/(2\cdot 3\cdot x)$ have? (Equivalently, how many irreducible components does $\Spec{}R$ have?)
hard.explain: In $\mathbb Z[x]$, the prime $(6x)=(2)\cap(3)\cap(x)$ factors as a meet of three minimal primes over $(6x)$: $(2)$, $(3)$, and $(x)$. In $\mathbb Z[x]/(6x)$ these become the three minimal primes, so $\Spec{}R$ has $3$ irreducible components.

questions.q: What is the Krull dimension of $\Spec k[x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4]$?
questions.explain: $\dim k[x_1,\dots,x_n]=n$, realised by the chain $(0)\subsetneq(x_1)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2,x_3)\subsetneq(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4)$. For $n=4$ this gives dimension $4$.

questions.q: What is $\dim\Spec\mathbb Z$?
questions.explain: The longest chain of primes in $\mathbb Z$ is $(0)\subsetneq(p)$ for any prime $p$ — length $1$. So $\dim\Spec\mathbb Z=1$; geometrically it is a 'curve' indexed by primes plus a generic point.

questions.q: For a finite-type scheme $X$ over a field $k$, the dimension of $X$ at a closed point $x\in X$ is:
choices: the Krull dimension of the local ring $\mathcal O_{X,x}$
the number of generators of the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m_x$
questions.explain: Dimension is a local invariant: $\dim_x X = \dim \mathcal O_{X,x}$, the Krull dimension of the local ring at $x$. The global $\dim X$ is the supremum over closed points (on a finite-type scheme over a field).

hard.q: What is $\dim\Spec\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2]$?
hard.explain: For a Noetherian ring $A$, $\dim A[x]=\dim A + 1$. Starting from $\dim\mathbb Z = 1$: $\dim\mathbb Z[x_1]=2$, $\dim\mathbb Z[x_1,x_2]=3$. A maximal chain is $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x_1)\subsetneq(p,x_1,x_2)$ for any prime $p$.

hard.q: Let $X=\Spec(k[x,y]/(xy))$ be the union of two coordinate axes. Which statement is TRUE?
choices: $\dim X$ is undefined because $X$ is not irreducible
hard.explain: The two irreducible components are the axes $V(x)$ and $V(y)$, each a copy of $\mathbb A^1_k$ of dimension $1$. So $\dim X=\max=1$, and since both components have the same dimension, $X$ is equidimensional.

hard.q: For the projective space $\mathbb P^n_k$, which assertion is correct?
choices: $\dim\mathbb P^n_k=n+1$ because of the extra point at infinity
$\dim\mathbb P^n_k=n$; each affine chart $D_+(x_i)\cong\mathbb A^n_k$ has dimension $n$ and gluing preserves local dimension
$\dim\mathbb P^n_k$ depends on the choice of homogeneous coordinates
hard.explain: Dimension is a local notion. $\mathbb P^n_k$ is covered by $n+1$ open affine charts each isomorphic to $\mathbb A^n_k$, so $\dim\mathbb P^n_k=n$. The fact that global sections are just $k$ is irrelevant to dimension (and is what distinguishes projective from affine).

questions.q: In $\Spec\mathbb Z[x]$, the closure of the prime $(x)$ equals:
choices: the 'axis' $V(x) = \{\mathfrak p : x\in\mathfrak p\}$, e.g. all primes of the form $(x,p)$ or $(x)$
questions.explain: The closure of a prime $\mathfrak p$ is $V(\mathfrak p)=\{\mathfrak q\supseteq\mathfrak p\}$. For $\mathfrak p=(x)$, these are all primes containing $x$, which are exactly $(x)$ itself together with all $(x,p)$ for $p$ prime — the entire 'vertical axis' of $\Spec\mathbb Z[x]$.

questions.q: A generic point of an irreducible closed subset $Z\subseteq\Spec{}R$ is:
choices: the unique point $\eta\in Z$ whose closure is all of $Z$
the set of all maximal ideals in $Z$
questions.explain: Every irreducible closed subset $Z$ of a scheme has a unique generic point $\eta$ with $\overline{\{\eta\}}=Z$. For $Z=V(\mathfrak p)$ with $\mathfrak p$ prime minimal with that property, the generic point is exactly $\mathfrak p$ itself.

questions.q: How many generic points does the scheme $\Spec(k[x,y]/(xy))$ have? (Count minimal primes.)
questions.explain: The minimal primes of $k[x,y]/(xy)$ are $(x)$ and $(y)$, one per irreducible component (the two coordinate axes). Each is the generic point of its component, so the scheme has $2$ generic points.

hard.q: Let $\mathfrak p\leadsto\mathfrak q$ denote specialization (i.e. $\mathfrak p\subseteq\mathfrak q$). Which statement about specialization in $\Spec\mathbb Z[x]$ is correct?
choices: Specialization is symmetric: $\mathfrak p\leadsto\mathfrak q$ implies $\mathfrak q\leadsto\mathfrak p$
Closed points can specialize to generic points
$(p,x)\leadsto(0)$ because the zero ideal is the smallest prime
hard.explain: Specialization flows from smaller (more generic) to larger (more special): $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x-a)$ gives a chain of specializations from the generic point of the whole spectrum, through the height-$1$ prime $(p)$, down to a closed point. Specialization is antisymmetric, not symmetric; closed points are terminal.

hard.q: In $\Spec\mathbb Z[x]$, what is the maximum length of a specialization chain $\mathfrak p_0\subsetneq\mathfrak p_1\subsetneq\cdots\subsetneq\mathfrak p_n$? (Give $n$, the number of strict inclusions.)
hard.explain: $\dim\mathbb Z[x]=2$, achieved by e.g. $(0)\subsetneq(p)\subsetneq(p,x-a)$: three primes, two strict inclusions. So the longest specialization chain has length $n=2$.

hard.q: Which claim about generic points and open dense subsets is TRUE in a Noetherian irreducible scheme $X$ with generic point $\eta$?
choices: An open subset $U\subseteq X$ is dense iff $\eta\in U$
$\eta$ lies in every closed subset of $X$
$\eta$ is isolated in the Zariski topology
hard.explain: Since $\overline{\{\eta\}}=X$, a closed set $Z\subsetneq X$ cannot contain $\eta$. Equivalently, an open $U$ is dense (its complement is a proper closed set) iff $\eta\in U$. This is why 'generic' properties — those that hold on a dense open — are exactly the ones that hold at $\eta$.

residue-field-at-prime.title: Residue fields and evaluation at points

questions.q: The residue field $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$ at a prime $\mathfrak p\subset R$ is defined as:
choices: $\operatorname{Frac}(R/\mathfrak p)$, equivalently $R_{\mathfrak p}/\mathfrak p R_{\mathfrak p}$
questions.explain: $\kappa(\mathfrak p)=\operatorname{Frac}(R/\mathfrak p)$, or equivalently the quotient of the local ring $R_{\mathfrak p}$ by its maximal ideal. For a maximal $\mathfrak p$, $R/\mathfrak p$ is already a field, but at non-maximal primes you need the fraction field.

questions.q: At the generic point $(0)\in\Spec\mathbb Z[x]$, the residue field is:
questions.explain: $\kappa((0))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z[x]/(0))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z[x])=\mathbb Q(x)$, the field of rational functions in one variable over $\mathbb Q$.

questions.q: At the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m=(2,x^2+x+1)\subset\mathbb Z[x]$, the residue field is $\mathbb Z[x]/(2,x^2+x+1)\cong\mathbb F_2[x]/(x^2+x+1)$. How many elements does $\kappa(\mathfrak m)$ have?
questions.explain: $x^2+x+1$ has no root in $\mathbb F_2$ (check $x=0,1$ both give $1$), so it is irreducible. Hence $\kappa(\mathfrak m)=\mathbb F_2[x]/(x^2+x+1)=\mathbb F_4$, a field of $4$ elements.

hard.q: Consider $f=x^2+1\in\mathbb Z[x]$, regarded as a 'function' on $\Spec\mathbb Z[x]$. At which of the following points does $f$ vanish, in the sense $f\in\mathfrak p$?
choices: Every prime of $\mathbb Z[x]$, because $f$ is monic
hard.explain:
'$f$ vanishes at $\mathfrak p$' means $f\in\mathfrak p$, equivalently $f\equiv 0$ in $\kappa(\mathfrak p)$. At $\mathfrak m=(5,x-2)$: $\kappa(\mathfrak m)=\mathbb F_5$ and $f\mapsto 2^2+1=5\equiv 0$, so yes. At $(3,x-1)$: $f\mapsto 2
ot\equiv 0$. At $(0)$: $\kappa((0))=\mathbb Q(x)$ and $f=x^2+1
e 0$, so $f
otin(0)$.

hard.q: A $k$-point of a $k$-scheme $X$ — a morphism $\Spec{}k\to X$ — corresponds to:
choices: a closed point $x$ together with an embedding $\kappa(x)\hookrightarrow k$
hard.explain: A morphism $\Spec{}k\to X$ picks out a point $x\in X$ and, since its image lies in the residue field, a $k$-algebra map $\kappa(x)\to k$, i.e. an embedding $\kappa(x)\hookrightarrow k$. When $k$ is algebraically closed and $\kappa(x)=k$ this recovers 'classical points', but in general the extra choice of embedding matters (Galois conjugate points).

hard.q: In $\mathbb Z[x]$, consider the maximal ideal $\mathfrak m=(p)$ of the height-$1$ prime $(p)\subset\mathbb Z[x]$ — i.e. the prime $(p)$ itself, height $1$, not a closed point. The residue field $\kappa((p))=\mathbb F_p(x)$. What is its transcendence degree over $\mathbb F_p$?
hard.explain: $\kappa((p))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb Z[x]/(p))=\operatorname{Frac}(\mathbb F_p[x])=\mathbb F_p(x)$. The transcendence degree of $\mathbb F_p(x)$ over $\mathbb F_p$ is $1$ — one transcendental generator $x$. (This matches the intuition that $(p)$ is a 'curve' in $\Spec\mathbb Z[x]$.)

questions.q: A locally ringed space is a pair $(X,\mathcal O_X)$ where $\mathcal O_X$ is a sheaf of rings such that:
choices: every stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a local ring
every stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a field
$\mathcal O_X$ has global sections equal to $\mathbb Z$
questions.explain: By definition, 'locally ringed' means every stalk is a local ring (a commutative ring with a unique maximal ideal). Stalks being fields is the much stronger condition that defines a 'sheaf of fields', not the right notion for schemes.

questions.q: A morphism of locally ringed spaces $(f, f^\sharp)\colon(X,\mathcal O_X)\to(Y,\mathcal O_Y)$ must satisfy, in addition to being a morphism of ringed spaces, that for each $x\in X$ the induced stalk map:
choices: $\mathcal O_{Y,f(x)}\to\mathcal O_{X,x}$ sends the maximal ideal into the maximal ideal (is a local homomorphism)
questions.explain: The extra condition is that each induced stalk map is a local ring homomorphism: $f^\sharp_x(\mathfrak m_{f(x)})\subseteq\mathfrak m_x$. Without this, maps between $\Spec$s would not correspond to ring homomorphisms in the right direction.

questions.q: A scheme is a locally ringed space that is:
choices: locally isomorphic (as a locally ringed space) to $\Spec{}R$ for some commutative ring $R$
globally isomorphic to $\Spec{}R$ for a single $R$
questions.hint: A scheme $X$ is a locally ringed space such that every point has an open neighborhood $U$ with $(U,\mathcal O_X|_U)\cong(\Spec{}R,\mathcal O_{\Spec{}R})$ for some ring $R$…
questions.explain: A scheme $X$ is a locally ringed space such that every point has an open neighborhood $U$ with $(U,\mathcal O_X|_U)\cong(\Spec{}R,\mathcal O_{\Spec{}R})$ for some ring $R$ (depending on $U$). Globally isomorphic to a single $\Spec{}R$ is the stronger 'affine' condition.

hard.q: Consider the ring map $\iota\colon k\hookrightarrow k[x]_{(x)}$ (inclusion of $k$ into the localization at $(x)$). The induced map on spectra sends the unique point of $\Spec{}k[x]_{(x)}\cong\{(0),(x)\}$ to a point of $\Spec{}k=\{*\}$. Which stalk map fails to be local if we mistakenly use a non-local ring homomorphism here?
choices: Any $k$-algebra map $k\to k[x]_{(x)}$ is automatically local
The map $k\to k[x]_{(x)}$ is local (the zero ideal of $k$ maps into $(x)k[x]_{(x)}$); the locality condition is automatic because $k$ is a field
The map is never local because $k$ is smaller than $k[x]_{(x)}$
Locality fails whenever the target has more than one prime
hard.explain: For a ring map $\phi\colon A\to B$ with $A$ local (maximal ideal $\mathfrak m_A$) and $B$ local (maximal ideal $\mathfrak m_B$), $\phi$ is local iff $\phi(\mathfrak m_A)\subseteq\mathfrak m_B$. When $A=k$ is a field, $\mathfrak m_A=(0)$ and $\phi(0)=0\in\mathfrak m_B$ automatically — locality is free. The subtlety only appears when $A$ has a non-zero maximal ideal that could map to a unit.

hard.q: Let $X$ be the topological space $\{\eta,s\}$ with $\{s\}$ closed (so $\eta$ is generic). Equip it with a sheaf $\mathcal O$ such that $\mathcal O(X)=R$, $\mathcal O(\{\eta\})=K$ where $R$ is a DVR with fraction field $K$, and restriction is the localization. Is $(X,\mathcal O)$ a scheme?
choices: Yes — it is canonically isomorphic to $\Spec{}R$
No — the stalk at $s$ is not local
No — a DVR has $3$ primes, not $2$
hard.explain: A DVR $R$ has exactly two primes: $(0)$ and the unique maximal $\mathfrak m$. So $\Spec{}R=\{(0),\mathfrak m\}$ with $\{\mathfrak m\}$ closed and $\{(0)\}$ generic, and $\mathcal O(X)=R$, $\mathcal O(\{(0)\})=\operatorname{Frac}R=K$. The described $(X,\mathcal O)$ matches $\Spec{}R$ exactly, and the stalks are $R_{\mathfrak m}=R$ and $R_{(0)}=K$, both local.

hard.q: Which of the following is an example of a ringed space that is NOT a locally ringed space?
choices: $(\Spec\mathbb Z,\mathcal O)$ with its usual structure sheaf
A point $\{*\}$ equipped with the ring $\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z$ (so the unique stalk is $\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z$, which has two maximal ideals)
An affine line over $k$ with its usual structure sheaf
A projective line over $k$ with its usual structure sheaf
hard.explain: $\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z$ is a product ring with two maximal ideals $\mathbb Z\times 0$ and $0\times\mathbb Z$ — it is not local. So the ringed space $(\{*\},\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z)$ fails to be locally ringed. All the other examples are schemes, hence locally ringed.

questions.q: For a graded ring $S=\bigoplus_{d\ge 0}S_d$, the underlying set of $\operatorname{Proj}S$ consists of:
choices: all homogeneous prime ideals $\mathfrak p\subset S$ not containing the irrelevant ideal $S_+=\bigoplus_{d>0}S_d$
questions.explain: $\operatorname{Proj}S=\{\mathfrak p\subset S\text{ homogeneous prime}:\mathfrak p\not\supseteq S_+\}$. Excluding those containing $S_+$ kills the 'irrelevant' locus that would otherwise give a cone point.

questions.q: For $S=k[x_0,x_1,\dots,x_n]$, match each piece of structure on $\operatorname{Proj}S=\mathbb P^n_k$ with its affine analog.
left: The subset where all of $x_0,\dots,x_n$ vanish
right: Empty — excluded by $\operatorname{Proj}$ since it would correspond to a prime containing $S_+$.
The $n+1$ charts $D_+(x_0),\dots,D_+(x_n)$, each isomorphic to $\mathbb A^n_k$.
Homogeneous primes of $S$ not containing the irrelevant ideal $S_+=(x_0,\dots,x_n)$.
questions.hint: $\operatorname{Proj}$ excludes primes containing the irrelevant ideal; each $D_+(x_i)$ is $\mathbb A^n_k$ obtained by dehomogenizing $x_i\mapsto 1$; the union covers $\mathbb P^n_k$.
questions.explain: $\operatorname{Proj}S$ takes only homogeneous primes not containing $S_+$, automatically excising the cone vertex. Each basic open $D_+(x_i)=\Spec((S_{x_i})_0)$ identifies with $\mathbb A^n_k$ — dehomogenize by setting $x_i=1$. The $n+1$ such opens cover $\mathbb P^n_k$ since any homogeneous prime not containing $S_+$ misses some $x_i$. This is the standard atlas presenting $\mathbb P^n$ as glued affine $n$-spaces.

questions.q: How many standard affine charts cover $\mathbb P^5_k=\operatorname{Proj}k[x_0,\dots,x_5]$?
questions.explain: $\mathbb P^n_k$ is covered by $n+1$ charts $D_+(x_i)$ for $i=0,\dots,n$. For $n=5$, that is $6$ charts.

hard.q: Consider the twisted cubic $C=\operatorname{Proj}k[s^3,s^2t,st^2,t^3]\subset\mathbb P^3_k$, the image of $\mathbb P^1_k\hookrightarrow\mathbb P^3_k$ via $[s:t]\mapsto[s^3:s^2t:st^2:t^3]$. Which description is correct?
choices: $C\cong\mathbb P^1_k$ via the parametrization, so $\dim C=1$
$C$ is empty because the ring is not a polynomial ring
hard.explain: The graded ring $k[s^3,s^2t,st^2,t^3]\subset k[s,t]$ is the Veronese subalgebra. Its Proj is the image of $\mathbb P^1_k\to\mathbb P^3_k$, which is an embedded copy of $\mathbb P^1_k$ — the twisted cubic. So $\dim C=1$.

hard.q: Let $S=k[x_0,x_1,x_2]$ and $I=(x_0^2+x_1^2-x_2^2)$. Then $\operatorname{Proj}(S/I)$ is:
choices: a smooth conic in $\mathbb P^2_k$ (over an algebraically closed $k$ of characteristic $\ne 2$), isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_k$
empty because $I$ contains a non-zero element
hard.explain: $x_0^2+x_1^2-x_2^2$ is a non-degenerate homogeneous quadratic form; over algebraically closed $k$ with $\operatorname{char}k\ne 2$ it defines a smooth conic in $\mathbb P^2_k$. All smooth plane conics are isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_k$ (e.g. via stereographic projection from any rational point).

hard.q: The global sections $H^0(\mathbb P^n_k,\mathcal O(d))$ for $d\ge 0$ is the degree-$d$ part of $k[x_0,\dots,x_n]$. What is $\dim_k H^0(\mathbb P^2_k,\mathcal O(3))$? (Number of degree-$3$ monomials in $3$ variables.)
hard.explain: The number of degree-$d$ monomials in $n+1$ variables is $\binom{n+d}{d}$. For $n=2$, $d=3$: $\binom{5}{3}=10$. The ten monomials are $x_0^3,x_0^2x_1,x_0^2x_2,x_0x_1^2,x_0x_1x_2,x_0x_2^2,x_1^3,x_1^2x_2,x_1x_2^2,x_2^3$.

fiber-over-point.title: Fibers of a morphism over a point

questions.q: For a morphism $f\colon X\to Y$ and $y\in Y$, the scheme-theoretic fiber $X_y$ is defined as:
choices: $f^{-1}(y)$ with the subspace topology, no extra structure
the image of $f$ restricted to a neighborhood of $y$
questions.explain: The fiber over $y$ is $X_y = X\times_Y \Spec\kappa(y)$, the base change of $X$ along the inclusion of the residue field at $y$. Its underlying set is $f^{-1}(y)$, but the fiber-product definition records scheme structure, multiplicities, and residue-field extensions.

questions.q: Consider the structural morphism $\Spec\mathbb Z[x]\to\Spec\mathbb Z$. The fiber over the closed point $(5)\in\Spec\mathbb Z$ is:
questions.explain: The fiber over $(p)$ is $\Spec(\mathbb Z[x]\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb F_p)=\Spec\mathbb F_p[x]=\mathbb A^1_{\mathbb F_p}$. For $p=5$ this is the affine line over $\mathbb F_5$.
questions.hint: Tensor $\mathbb Z[x]$ with the residue field $\kappa((5))=\mathbb F_5$.

questions.q: The generic fiber of a morphism $X\to Y$ (with $Y$ integral) is the fiber over:
choices: the generic point $\eta\in Y$, i.e. $X\times_Y\Spec{}K(Y)$ where $K(Y)$ is the function field
questions.explain: The generic fiber is $X_\eta = X\times_Y \Spec{}K(Y)$, the fiber over the generic point of $Y$. It carries all properties that hold on a dense open subset of $Y$; 'special fibers' are the fibers over closed points.

hard.q: Consider $f\colon\Spec\mathbb Z[i]\to\Spec\mathbb Z$. How many closed points does the fiber over $(5)\in\Spec\mathbb Z$ have?
hard.explain: Fiber over $(5)$ is $\Spec(\mathbb Z[i]\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb F_5)=\Spec\mathbb F_5[x]/(x^2+1)$. Over $\mathbb F_5$, $x^2+1=(x-2)(x+2)$ (since $2^2=4\equiv-1$), so the fiber is $\Spec(\mathbb F_5\times\mathbb F_5)$: two closed points. ($5$ splits in $\mathbb Z[i]$.)

hard.q: For the family $X=\Spec{}k[t,x]/(x^2-t)\to Y=\Spec{}k[t]$ (with $k$ algebraically closed of characteristic $\ne 2$), which statement correctly compares the fibers?
choices: All fibers are two reduced points
The fiber over $t=a\ne 0$ is two reduced closed points ($x=\pm\sqrt a$); the fiber over $t=0$ is a single non-reduced point $\Spec{}k[x]/(x^2)$
The generic fiber is two reduced points, but every closed-point fiber is empty
hard.hint: What happens to the two roots $\pm\sqrt{t}$ as $t\to 0$?
hard.explain: Over $t=a\ne 0$: $k[x]/(x^2-a)\cong k\times k$, giving two reduced closed points. Over $t=0$: $k[x]/(x^2)$, a single non-reduced length-$2$ fat point. This is the standard example of a family with a degenerate special fiber — the double cover of $\mathbb A^1$ ramified at $0$.

hard.q: Let $f\colon\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Z}\to\Spec\mathbb Z$. Which statement about the fibers is TRUE?
choices: The fiber over $(p)$ is $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb F_p}$; the generic fiber is $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Q}$
The generic fiber is empty because $(0)$ is not a closed point
The fiber over $(p)$ is $\mathbb A^1_{\mathbb F_p}$, not $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb F_p}$
hard.explain: Base change commutes with Proj: $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Z}\times_{\Spec\mathbb Z}\Spec\kappa((p))=\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb F_p}$, and similarly the generic fiber is $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Q}$. The fibers are genuine $\mathbb P^1$'s over each residue field — the 'arithmetic surface' $\mathbb P^1_{\mathbb Z}$ is a family of projective lines parametrized by $\Spec\mathbb Z$.
